w:r.w:t: Banking - Payments - Tax & E-filing - E-commerce - Public API & Webhooks
Vol. 1 BC-05, BC-06, BC-11, BC-19; Vol. 2 CTX-05 Banking, CTX-06 Payments, CTX-10 Tax, CTX-16 Integration Gateway; Vol. 3 event bus & anti-corruption layer pattern; Vol. 4 banking/tax schemas
Vol. 6 Security Architecture; Vol. 7 Infrastructure & Deployment; Vol. 8 Testing & QA; Vol. 9 UI/UX & API Design

This volume designs the platform's integrations with the external systems identified in Vol. 1's system context (Section 4 personas, BC-05 Banking, BC-06 Online Payments, BC-11 Tax Compliance, BC-19 Integration & Ecosystem) and the C4 Level 1 diagram (Vol. 2 Section 4): bank feed providers, payment gateways, tax/e-filing services, e-commerce channels, and the platform's own outbound public API and webhook surface for third-party integrators. Every integration in this volume is owned by the Integration Gateway bounded context (CTX-16) or a directly adjacent context (Banking CTX-05, Payments CTX-06, Tax CTX-10) per Vol. 2's context map, and every external call crosses through an anti-corruption layer per Vol. 2 Section 2.2.
1.2 Why Integrations Get Their Own Volume

Third-party financial integrations carry disproportionate operational risk relative to their apparent scope: they are the platform's least controllable dependency (rate limits, breaking API changes, downtime outside the platform's control), the most security-sensitive surface (credentials, tokenized payment data), and the hardest to test end-to-end before production. This volume exists to make every integration's failure mode, retry behavior, and data-ownership boundary explicit before implementation begins, rather than discovering them during an incident.
Every external integration sits behind an anti-corruption layer (ACL)

No third-party API shape (field names, enums, pagination style) ever leaks past the Integration Gateway module into core domain modules (Vol. 2 Section 3.3); the ACL translates to the platform's own domain events/DTOs.
Prefer webhooks over polling wherever the provider supports it
Reduces latency and API-quota consumption; polling is the documented fallback, not the default (Section 3, 6).
All inbound webhooks are verified before processing

Signature verification (HMAC or provider-specific scheme) is mandatory before a webhook payload touches any domain logic - unverified webhooks are rejected with 401, not silently ignored, so failures are visible in monitoring.
All integrations are idempotent on both inbound and outbound sides

Inbound: webhook processing dedupes on provider event ID. Outbound: every mutating call to a third party carries an idempotency key (Vol. 3 Section 5) so retries after a timeout cannot double-charge or double-submit.
Integration failures degrade gracefully, they do not corrupt financial state

A failed tax-calculation call blocks invoice send with a clear error rather than posting an invoice with an unknown tax amount; a failed bank-feed sync leaves reconciliation state unchanged rather than partially applying transactions.
Outbound event delivery uses the transactional outbox pattern

A domain event and the database write that caused it commit in the same transaction; a separate relay process publishes to Kafka, guaranteeing no event is lost even if Kafka is briefly unavailable (Section 8).

Direct bank-by-bank integration is not viable at MVP scale (thousands of individual bank connections). The platform integrates with a bank-data aggregator (Plaid/Yodlee/TrueLayer-class, region-dependent - see Section 11 vendor evaluation) that itself maintains the individual bank connections, exposing a single normalized API and webhook stream to the platform.
User->>Bank: authenticate directly with bank (credentials never touch platform)
GW->>GW: create banking.bank_accounts record via Banking module API
User bank credentials never pass through the platform's own servers - this is a deliberate security and compliance boundary (formalized in Vol. 6) that also reduces the platform's liability surface.
Provider supports push notifications for new transactions
Aggregator calls the Integration Gateway's webhook endpoint on new transaction availability; Gateway then pulls the transaction batch via API and publishes BankTransactionImported (Vol.2 Section 10)
Provider/bank connection does not support webhooks, or webhook delivery has failed repeatedly
Poll every 4 hours per Vol.3 Section 9 background job design; poll frequency is configurable per tenant edition (Enterprise tenants may get more frequent polling)
Normalize provider-specific transaction categories into the platform's own category taxonomy before they reach the Banking module.
Normalize currency/amount representation (some providers return minor units, others decimal) into the platform's NUMERIC(19,4) convention (Vol. 4 Section 8).
Translate provider-specific error codes into the platform's namespaced error codes (Vol. 3 Section 4.3).
Shield the rest of the platform from provider API version changes - only the ACL adapter needs to change when the provider ships a breaking API update.

The platform integrates with a small set of tokenizing payment gateways (Stripe-class for card/ACH in US/EU markets, Razorpay-class for India, expandable per Section 11) via the Payments bounded context (CTX-06), which Vol. 2 Section 2.1 already scoped as an independently-deployable service from Release 1 given its inherent dependency on volatile third-party APIs.

The platform never stores raw card numbers. All card capture happens via the gateway's hosted fields or client-side tokenization SDK, so card data goes directly from the customer's browser to the gateway, and the platform only ever handles an opaque payment token/method ID. This keeps the platform at PCI SAQ-A (Vol. 1 Section 10), the lowest self-assessment tier, rather than SAQ-D.
Payment link generation and webhook confirmation
AR->>Pay: request payment link for Invoice INV-0042
Pay->>GW: create payment intent (idempotency key = invoiceId)
GW-->>Pay: payment intent + hosted checkout URL
AR->>Cust: send invoice with payment link (Vol.1 VS-01)
Cust->>GW: completes payment on hosted checkout
Pay->>Pay: dedupe on gateway event ID (Principle I4)
Pay->>AR: publish PaymentRecorded (via event bus, Vol.2 Section 10)
AR->>AR: apply payment to invoice (Vol.2 Section 9.1 flow continues)

For recurring invoices (Vol. 1 BC-02, Vol. 3 Section 9 scheduled job), the Payments service stores a reusable payment-method token (not the platform's own credential) and initiates a server-side charge on the recurrence date, with the same idempotency-key and webhook-confirmation pattern as Section 4.3. A failed auto-charge triggers a dunning workflow (Automation module, CTX-14) rather than silent failure.

(a) Build an in-house tax rate/rules engine, (b) Integrate a specialized tax-calculation vendor (Avalara/TaxJar-class), (c) Hybrid: simple flat-rate calculation built in-house for MVP, vendor integration for multi-jurisdiction complexity in R2/R3

Hybrid: in-house single-jurisdiction flat/tiered rate calculation for MVP (Vol.1 Section 6 BC-11 MVP scope), with a pluggable tax-provider interface designed from day one so a specialized vendor can be integrated in R2/R3 without changing the Receivables/Payables call sites

Tax jurisdiction rules (especially US sales tax nexus and multi-state rates) are notoriously complex and change frequently; building this in-house beyond simple cases is a poor use of engineering effort relative to buying it from a vendor that specializes in exactly this. The pluggable interface (Section 5.2) avoids a costly rework when that vendor integration is added.
// Selected per-tenant via configuration (Vol.3 Section 10 feature flag pattern)
Batch export to IRS-compatible format (IRIS) or a filing intermediary (Tax1099/Yerli-class); asynchronous job, not real-time API call
Where the tax vendor (Section 5.1) offers filing-as-a-service, delegate to it; otherwise generate a filing-ready report for manual submission
Integration with GSTN-compatible filing API or intermediary, similar batch-export pattern
Webhook for orders; scheduled push for stock levels (batched to respect rate limits)
Amazon SP-API; polling required for several resource types per API constraints
Includes shipment tracking and payout/settlement reconciliation per Vol.1 research (2026 Walmart integration feature)
E-commerce order import to Sales Order/Invoice
GW->>GW: verify signature, dedupe on channel order ID
GW->>INV: check/reserve stock for order line items
GW->>AR: create Sales Order (channel = Shopify, external ref = order ID)
AR->>AR: optionally auto-convert to Invoice per tenant configuration

Note over GW,Ch: On stock update elsewhere (Vol.2 StockAdjusted event),
Gateway pushes updated stock levels back to Channel (rate-limited batch)
If a channel order references a SKU not found in the platform's item master, the order is imported in a Needs Review state rather than silently dropped or auto-created as a new item.
If stock is insufficient at import time, the order still imports (the sale already happened on the channel) but flags a backorder/oversell warning for the Inventory Manager (PER-06).
Channel-side price/tax figures are treated as informational; the platform recalculates tax via the Tax module (Section 5) as the authoritative figure for financial posting.

This is the platform acting as the upstream integration provider for third-party developers and partner apps - the mirror image of Sections 3-6. It builds directly on Vol. 3 Section 5's API conventions.
Tenant/developer registers a webhook URL per event type (subset of Vol.2 Section 10's domain event catalog, e.g. invoice.sent, payment.recorded)
Every outbound webhook payload is signed with an HMAC secret unique per subscription, verifiable by the receiving third party (mirrors Principle I3 for inbound)
At-least-once, with exponential backoff retry (up to 24 hours) and a dead-letter queue for permanently failing endpoints, surfaced in a developer-facing delivery log
Best-effort ordering per aggregate (same partitioning as the internal event bus, Vol.3 Section 8.1); receivers must not assume strict global ordering

Rate limits are enforced at the API Gateway (Vol. 2 Section 5) using the Redis-backed counters described in Vol. 3 Section 7.1, tiered by edition (Vol. 1 Section 9): Standard/Professional get a shared default quota, Enterprise tenants get a higher or dedicated quota. Limit responses follow standard 429 + Retry-After conventions.
A[Domain service writes entity change + outbox row in ONE DB transaction] --> B[(Postgres)]
B --> C[Outbox Relay Process - polls or uses logical replication/CDC]

This guarantees that a committed domain event (e.g. InvoiceSent) is never lost even if Kafka is briefly unreachable, and that no event is published for a database write that ultimately rolled back - both failure modes that a naive "publish then write" or "write then publish" approach would allow.
Calls to the external provider proceed normally; failures are counted in a rolling window

After a failure-rate threshold is crossed, calls fail fast without hitting the provider, protecting both the platform (no thread/connection exhaustion) and the already-struggling provider from additional load
After a cooldown period, a limited number of calls are allowed through to test recovery before fully closing the circuit again
Retries for up to 24h, then dead-letter
5 attempts, then flagged for manual review
No automatic retry on charge creation (to avoid double-charge risk) - relies on idempotency key + gateway's own idempotent handling instead
1 attempt; failure surfaces to user immediately
Component detail for the Integration Gateway, extending Vol. 2 Section 6's C4 Level 3 diagrams to this context, which Vol. 2 Section 2.1 already scoped as an independently-deployable service.

This is a starting evaluation frame, not a final vendor selection - final selection depends on target-market confirmation (Vol. 1 Section 13.2 open question on countries) and commercial negotiation outside this document's scope.
Bank coverage in target markets, webhook support, pricing per connected account, data normalization quality
PCI SAQ-A compatibility (Section 4.2), supported payment methods per market, payout/settlement reporting quality
Jurisdiction coverage, filing-as-a-service availability (Section 5.3), API latency (blocks invoice send, Vol.1 Section 2.4 P95 target)
Tax1099, Yerli, direct IRS IRIS, GSTN-certified providers
Direct government API access vs. intermediary, batch vs. real-time filing
Vol. 1 BC-05; Vol. 2 CTX-05; Vol. 4 banking.bank_transactions schema
Vol. 1 BC-06; Vol. 2 CTX-06 (scoped as independent service, Section 2.1)
Vol. 3 Section 5 API conventions; Vol. 2 Section 10 event catalog (outbound subset)
Vol. 3 Section 8.2 idempotent consumers; Vol. 1 Principle P4 (auditability - outbox guarantees no lost financial events)
Confirm target markets (Vol.1 Section 13.2 Q1) to finalize which bank aggregator and tax vendor candidates (Section 10) are actually in-region.

Confirm whether a payment gateway aggregator/orchestration layer (to support multiple gateways per region without duplicating integration work) is worth the added complexity at MVP, or whether a single gateway per initial market is acceptable.

Confirm risk tolerance for the Section 5.1 hybrid tax approach - is flat-rate MVP tax calculation acceptable to launch with, or does the first target market require multi-jurisdiction accuracy from day one?

On approval, Phase 6 begins: Security Architecture (Volume 6) - threat model, IAM design, encryption/credential-storage standards (referenced but not detailed in this volume's Section 9 Credential Store), and compliance control mapping (SOC2/GDPR/PCI) covering both the core platform and every integration surface defined here.
